name: Sulfur

searchTerm: blue winged olive BWO Baetis mayfly

name: Hendrickson
notes: First major mayfly hatch of the year up north. Kicks off dry-fly season.

name: March Brown
notes: Sporadic emergence rather than dense hatches. Big bite when it comes off.

name: Grannom Caddis
notes: Mother's-Day caddis on many rivers. Bites both surface and subsurface — fish a dropper.

notes: Swim-fast nymphs trout key on. Great pre-hatch swing pattern.

name: Salmonfly (Western)
notes: The biggest mayfly hatch in the West — the 'salmonfly' is actually a stonefly, 3 in long. Hatch moves upstream like a wave; trout key in for 7-10 days. Big Hole + Madison + Yellowstone are the legendary salmonfly waters.

name: PMD (Western)
searchTerm: pale morning dun PMD Ephemerella excrucians
notes: Pale Morning Dun — the iconic Western mayfly. Henry's Fork PMD blanket hatch is bucket-list. Mornings, fish slowly on long leaders + 6X tippet.

name: Golden Stone (Western)
notes: Trails the salmonfly hatch by ~2 weeks and overlaps with PMDs. Big dry-fly opportunity all summer; nymphs work year-round.

name: Callibaetis (Western stillwater)
notes: Western stillwater mayfly. Mornings on calm reservoirs and spring creeks. Henry's Lake + Hebgen + Strawberry are textbook callibaetis water.

name: Skwala Stonefly (Western)
notes: Early-season Western hatch — late March / early April on the Bitterroot is the signature. Smaller olive cousin of salmonfly; trout get drowned-out aggressive on the first warm afternoons.

name: Mahogany Dun (Western)
notes: Fall Western mayfly — overlaps with BWOs. Dark cinnamon body; trout key on them on overcast Sept/Oct afternoons.

name: Green Drake (Western)
notes: Henry's Fork green drake hatch is one of fly fishing's iconic events. Big mayfly — trout move for it. Late June into July, afternoons.

name: October Caddis (Western)
notes: Last big dry-fly opportunity of the year. Late-September into mid-October — fall colors + big orange caddis on the surface. Big browns key on them before the spawn.

name: Trico (Western tailwaters)
notes: Tailwater trico hatch — Missouri below Holter, Bighorn below Afterbay. Morning spinner falls; 7X tippet, picky fish. Same insect as Eastern trico, more reliable on Western tailwaters.

name: Quill Gordon
notes: The first mayfly hatch of the year in the Smokies. Cold, often drizzly early-spring afternoons. Trout aren't selective yet — close-enough patterns work.

name: Light Cahill
notes: Late-evening hatch through warm summer months. Pale cream body + light wings — easy to spot in low light. Spinner falls right at dark.

name: Slate Drake
notes: Late-summer / fall counterpart to Isonychia bicolor. Crawls out on rocks to emerge — fish wets and emergers near banks at dusk.

name: Sulfur (MI rivers)
notes: Mid-May through June on the Au Sable + Manistee. Smaller than the South Holston version. Evening rises through twilight.

name: Brown Drake
notes: Late-June dusk hatch — Hex precursor on MI rivers; Henrys Fork bucket-list event. Spinner fall at last light is THE event. Window is narrow (~10 days).

name: Mother's Day Caddis
notes: Yellowstone + Madison legend — dense swarms in late April / early May right before runoff. Fish lose their minds. Often coincides with the holiday weekend.

name: Spruce Moth
notes: Late summer terrestrial — adults blown into the water from spruce/fir stands. Fish key on them HARD on the Madison + upper Yellowstone. Morning is best; calm water.

name: Pale Evening Dun (PED)
searchTerm: pale evening dun Ephemerella inermis PED
notes: Evening cousin of the PMD — pale yellow body, smaller. Henrys Fork + Bighorn evening fishing through summer. Trout get picky as light fades — go small.

name: Flav (Small Western Green Drake)
notes: Smaller follow-up to the Western Green Drake. July-August afternoons. Olive body, mottled wings — important on the Madison + Henrys Fork.

name: Pink Albert
notes: Henrys Fork specialty — pinkish body, evening rise on flat water. The picky-fish hatch. 7X tippet, drag-free or nothing.

name: Hydropsyche / Spotted Sedge
notes: Most-common summer caddis across both Western + Eastern tailwaters. Larva (green rock-worm) is a year-round nymph go-to. Adults fall hard in evening.

name: Little Yellow Stone (Western)
notes: Western counterpart to Yellow Sally. Summer afternoon hatch — fish all day on most Western freestone. Often blanket-hatch on the Madison.

name: Mysis Shrimp (tailwater staple)
notes: Trophy-fish food on the Frying Pan (Toilet Bowl), Bighorn, and Green. Drift through the dam outflow — that's where the protein is. Mysis are 0.5-1 in long, translucent white.

name: Scud (tailwater staple)
notes: Year-round protein staple on tailwaters. Dead-drift on the bottom — split-shot, indicator. Pink scuds work where rainbows have seen everything.

name: Sowbug / Cress Bug
searchTerm: sowbug cress bug fly pattern tailwater
notes: Year-round tailwater protein, especially Arkansas + Pennsylvania limestone. Sister-fly to scud but flatter, drabber. Bounce them along bottom seams.

name: San Juan Worm (universal nymph)
flies: Bead-Head Green Weenie — PA-born inchworm/caterpillar suggestion; deadly on the Little Juniata
searchTerm: san juan worm squirmy worm fly pattern
notes: Cheating? Maybe. Catches fish? Always. Fish it on a tight-line under an indicator anywhere worms wash in — spring runoff, post-rain, tailwaters.

notes: The Southern Appalachian fly canon — these patterns are the regional DNA shared across TN, NC, GA, KY, and southwestern VA. The Tellico Nymph and Yallerhammer are the two most distinctive — both predate modern fly fishing and trace back to the Cherokee and Scotch-Irish settlers of the southern mountains. Fred Hall (Smokies) and Roger Lowe (Waynesville NC) gave us the modern high-floaters; Frank Coffey gave us the latex-bodied stonefly out of the old Dayco plant in Waynesville. Yellow + Adams + bushy hackle is the recipe for all of them.

name: Flint River Shoal Bass (uniquely Georgia)

searchTerm: Flint River shoal bass Stealth Bomber Kent Edmonds Georgia
notes: The Flint River shoal bass is endemic to the Apalachicola-Chattahoochee-Flint drainage and almost nowhere else on earth. They live in current over rock shoals and eat like smallmouth in moving water — crayfish, baitfish, hellgrammites. The Stealth Bomber (Kent Edmonds, GA) is the most distinctly Georgia fly ever tied: came out of an attempt to replicate Turk's Tarantula with foam after a Henry's Fork trip; the way it dives then flutters back to the surface is what makes it deadly. If you fish one Georgia river, fish this one.

Almost entirely non-hatch streamer fishing — stripers stage in tidewater + below the falls, key on alewife, blueback herring, and shad. 8-10 weight rods, intermediate to type-5 sink tips, big white/chartreuse flies. Surface action when stripers push bait against the banks.

Big articulated streamers + eggs + junk + outsized stoneflies. Treat it like a trophy private water with public access.

Same fly canon either way: baitfish profiles (EP, Game Changer, Clouser, Deceiver), crabs (Merkin, Strong-Arm) on the flats, surface (Crease, Gurgler) when fish blow up on bait.

Peak July-August.

notes: Michigan is one of the world's premier fly-rod carp destinations. The Grand Traverse Bay "golden bones" of Lake Michigan get sight-fished on shallow flats; Beaver Island is a legendary destination; Lake St. Clair has another massive flats fishery; and warmwater rivers like the Huron, Flint, Shiawassee, and Kalamazoo all hold them. The season for carp on the flats of Grand Traverse Bay and Lake Michigan ranges from mid-May through mid-July. Crayfish and nymph patterns are the preferred patterns — these same flies are also preferred by the smallmouth bass.

Pike are everywhere in the U.P. and the northern lower. 9-10 weight rods, wire bite tippet, big articulated flies (8-12 inches), and you strip strikes. Spring (post-ice through pre-spawn) and fall (water cools, fish gorge) are peak; summer can be tough with weeds + warm water.

Schultz Outfitters fishes the Huron (home water), Flint, Shiawassee, and Kalamazoo — all produce exceptional smallmouth bass, pike, and carp. The lower Manistee, lower Au Sable, St. Joseph, and Grand all hold strong populations too. Crayfish patterns dominate; topwater poppers in summer. Many of these same flies cross to carp on the same waters.

name: Leonard Halladay & the Boardman River Classics
flies: Adams (Leonard Halladay, Mayfield MI, 1922) — the most famous dry fly in the world; tied near Mayfield Pond on the Boardman River at the request of Charles Adams. Gray body, grizzly+brown hackle, mallard wings, grizzly+brown hackle-fiber tails

searchTerm: Adams fly Halladay Mayfield Boardman Michigan dry fly origin
notes: The Adams was designed by Leonard Halladay of Mayfield, Michigan in 1922 at the request of his friend Charles Adams — tied near Mayfield Pond on the Boardman River. As Ed Van Put put it: "The Adams doesn't look exactly like anything but it looks enough like a lot of things that it just catches fish." The most famous dry fly in the world is a Michigan fly. Halladay also tied the Hair Stone and Michigamme in the same color scheme, plus the Female Adams with a yellow egg sack. Art Winnie of Traverse City gave us Joe's Hopper and the Whinnie Fore & Aft. Bob Smock Sr. is part of the same old-school northern MI tradition.

notes: Rusty Gates was central to the Au Sable's modern identity, alongside Ernie Schwiebert, Bob Linsenman, Carl Richards, and Doug Swisher. Gates developed a number of patterns that became standard Au Sable flies; Josh Greenberg still ties them at Gates Au Sable Lodge today. The Au Sable canon also includes Clarence Roberts of Onaway (Roberts Yellow Drake, late 1950s, with the parachute post built for one-eyed George Griffith — TU co-founder) and George Griffith himself (Griffith's Gnat). The vast majority of patterns tied at the Lodge are MI-specific, designed for this river system and its wild trout.

notes: Selective Trout (Doug Swisher and Carl Richards, 1971) is one of the most influential fly-fishing books ever written — born out of Michigan trout fishing. The No-Hackle Dun, Stillborn, and Hairwing Dun designs all came from this MI-rooted 'scientific' school. Ernie Schwiebert (Matching the Hatch) and Bob Linsenman (Modern Streamers for Trophy Trout, with Galloup) are part of the same lineage. The Michigan school of dry-fly design changed how the world fishes mayfly hatches.

Heavy 11-12 wt rods with bite tippet.

flies: Crazy Charlie (Bob Nauheim / Charlie Smith) — THE foundational bonefish pattern; pink + tan + chartreuse
Gotcha (Jim McVay, Andros) — Bahamian classic, now Keys staple
Bonefish Clouser / Gotcha-Clouser — weighted Clouser-style bonefish fly
Bonefish Bitters / Pop's Bitters (Pop Smith) — crabby-shrimpy hybrid
Christmas Island Special — Pacific classic that works in FL
CXI Special (Randall Kaufmann) — Christmas Island Special variant
Mini Puff — small subdued bonefish fly
Veverka's Mantis Shrimp — mantis shrimp imitation
Kwan (Sam Root) — Biscayne Bay-born; bonefish + redfish workhorse
Borski's Bonefish Slider (Tim Borski) — hopping presentation
Borski Chernobyl Crab — small flats crab
Bunny Bone / Bunny Gotcha — rabbit-strip Gotcha variant
Greg's Flats Fly — Greg Vinci flats pattern
Pillow Talk — soft Keys bonefish pattern
Brewer's Amber Shrimp — amber shrimp profile
Polar Charlie — flash Crazy Charlie variant
Tasty Toad (Harry Spear) — Keys flats classic
searchTerm: Florida bonefish fly Keys Crazy Charlie Gotcha Kwan
notes: Bonefishing in the FL Keys + Biscayne Bay started with the Crazy Charlie (Bob Nauheim, late 1970s) — every bonefish fly built since traces back to it. Today's box: a few Crazy Charlies (pink + tan + chartreuse), Gotchas, Kwans, and Bitters for the flats range from shallow tailing reds to deeper schools. 8-9 wt rod, 10-12 ft leader to 12-15 lb fluoro tippet.

name: Florida Permit Flies
flies: Merkin Crab (Del Brown) — THE permit fly; the standard against which all others are judged
Bauer's Flats Crab — modern flats-crab variant
EP Crab (Enrico Puglisi) — fiber-based crab profile
EP Campeche Crab — Yucatan-school crab refined for FL
Avalon Permit Fly — beaded weight + rubber legs, Cuba/Belize-school
Flexo Crab / Alphlexo Crab — modern foam-shell crab
Strong Arm Crab — Harry Spear-style spartina-grass crab
RIO's Liquid Courage — modern permit fly
Casa Blanca Raghead Crab — Yucatan variant
searchTerm: Florida permit fly Merkin Crab Del Brown
notes: Permit are the holiest grail of flats fly fishing — incredibly spooky, demanding of perfect presentations. Del Brown's Merkin Crab (1979) is the universal answer when in doubt. Carry weights from 1/0 to 4 to match the depth + the wind. 9-10 wt rod, 15-20 lb fluoro tippet.

name: Florida Snook Flies
flies: Seaducer — FL Keys classic, palmered hackle
DT Special — Sarasota beach snook standard
Lefty's Deceiver (Lefty Kreh) — universal saltwater profile
Gartside Gurgler (Jack Gartside) — the topwater go-to for snook
EP Peanut Butter (Enrico Puglisi) — small baitfish profile
Marabou Madness (Chico Fernandez) — marabou snook pattern
Schminnow / Crystal Schminnow (Norm Zeigler) — Sanibel beach snook fly; arguably the most-effective beach snook fly ever tied
Floating Minnow — red head, marabou tail; old-school beach snook pattern
Chartreuse + White Clouser — universal beach snook
Glass Minnow / Glades Minnow — translucent baitfish
searchTerm: Florida snook fly Schminnow Seaducer Sanibel beach
notes: Snook fishing has two flavors: beach snook (Sanibel + Sarasota, where the Schminnow rules) and mangrove backcountry (Bend-Backs + weedless Seaducers). Norm Zeigler's Schminnow on Sanibel is one of the most efficient saltwater fly inventions ever — beach-cruising snook eat it with abandon. 8-9 wt rod, 20 lb fluoro tippet.

name: Florida Redfish Flies
flies: Borski Slider (Tim Borski) — hopping flats presentation
Redfish Crack / Crack Fly — modern tailing-redfish standard
RIO Shrimp Tease — modern redfish shrimp
Estaz Krill / Estaz Shrimp — flash-bodied shrimp
Tutti Frutti — bright attractor redfish fly
Toad (in redfish colors with gold flash) — Toad variant
Spoon Fly (Drew Chicone-style) — flutter-and-fall spoon fly
Merkin Crab (small) — for tailing reds in spartina grass
VIP Popper — small popper for blow-up reds
searchTerm: Florida redfish fly Mosquito Lagoon Kwan Borski Slider
notes: Florida redfish are the most accessible saltwater fly target — they tail in shallow grass year-round on the Mosquito Lagoon, Indian River, and Tampa Bay flats. Spoon Fly + Kwan + Borski Slider are the three-fly box that catches them everywhere. Sight-fish tailing reds in 1-3 ft. 8 wt rod, 16-20 lb fluoro tippet.

name: Florida Sea Trout / Mackerel / Jacks Inshore Flies
scientific: Cynoscion nebulosus / Scomberomorus maculatus / Caranx spp.
flies: Surf Candy (Bob Popovics) — epoxy baitfish, glassy
Bob's Banger — popper for blow-up trout/jack
Beast Fleye (Popovics) — bigger profile for jacks + big fish
Game Changer / Finesse Game Changer (Blane Chocklett) — articulated baitfish
searchTerm: Florida inshore fly sea trout mackerel jacks Clouser
notes: Sea trout, Spanish mackerel, jacks, ladyfish, bluefish — the FL inshore mixed-bag list. Clouser Minnow + Surf Candy + Gartside Gurgler cover almost everything. Spanish mackerel chew leaders — use a short wire bite tippet when they're blitzing. 7-9 wt rod.

name: Florida Nearshore / Offshore Flies
flies: Cam Sigler's Tube Fly — billfish tube fly (sailfish + marlin)
Big Game Deceiver — large Deceiver in offshore baitfish colors
Sea Habit Deceiver (Popovics) — pushable big-water baitfish
Pole Dancer (Charlie Bisharat) — walks-the-dog surface slider
Big Red Shark Fly — bright red shark pattern
Ballyhoo imitation — Keys offshore baitfish profile
Hollow Fleye in mackerel / bluefish colors — pushable big baitfish
searchTerm: Florida offshore fly sailfish cobia king mackerel tube fly
notes: Offshore + nearshore fly fishing: Stuart sailfish, Keys offshore mahi + king mackerel, Gulf cobia spring run. Heavy 10-14 wt rods, fast sink lines, bite-tippet leaders. Tube flies for billfish (Cam Sigler's are the standard). Pole Dancers + Crease Flies for surface jacks.

name: Salmonfly (Western Giant Stone)
flies: Size 4–8 Pat's Rubber Legs (brown/black, brown/coffee, coffee/black)
Size 4–8 Schroeder's Parachute Hopper as ovipositing adult
notes: Biggest mayfly-class hatch in North America (it's a stonefly but acts like a mayfly). Timing follows snowmelt — late June on the Big Hole, early July on the Madison. Trout key on the giant dries; trophy browns lose caution.

name: Golden Stone
flies: Size 8 Yellow Sally Stimulator (smaller golden cousin)
Size 6–10 Pat's Rubber Legs (coffee/black, golden/black, tan)
notes: Overlaps Salmonfly hatch by 1–2 weeks. Same playbook + smaller flies. Tan/golden coloration. Bug ovipositions visible on the surface.

name: Yellow Sally Stonefly
notes: Smaller cousin of Salmonfly/Golden Stone — yellow body, prolific summer afternoon emergence. Great hopper-dropper anchor or stand-alone dry.

name: Skwala Stonefly (Early Spring)
notes: First major Western dry-fly hatch of the year. Pre-runoff window when little else is happening. Bitterroot March-April most famous.

name: PMD (Pale Morning Dun)
searchTerm: pale morning dun PMD Ephemerella inermis
notes: Western tailwater + spring-creek signature. Peak hatch midday; rusty spinner fall in evening. Trout key on emergers and cripples in the film.

name: Trico (Western)
flies: Size 20–24 Trico Spinner (poly wing, black body, white wing)
notes: Late-summer Western morning game. 7–10 AM spinner fall. Tiny flies + 7X tippet + fine technique. Bighorn + Missouri Craig + Silver Creek legendary.

name: Mahogany Dun
notes: Late-summer / fall Western mayfly. Often last good dry-fly window before snow.

name: Blue Wing Olive (Western)
notes: Year-round small mayfly — cool overcast days produce the best dry-fly windows. Fall + spring peaks. Western fall BWOs include the larger flavilinea (Small Western Green Drake — different bug, related).

name: Fall BWO
notes: Fall BWO generation — best on cool cloudy fall afternoons. Same patterns as spring BWO.

name: Green Drake (Western)
notes: Western Green Drake — biggest mayfly. Henry's Fork legendary (Last Chance hatch). Trophy trout move out of cover for them.

name: Mother's Day Caddis
notes: Spring blanket hatch — first big bug fest after winter. Often overlaps muddy runoff; fish where the water clears.

name: October Caddis
notes: Last big dry-fly hatch of the year. Orange body + huge size. Browns get aggressive pre-spawn.

name: Western March Brown
notes: Early-spring Western mayfly. Different species from Eastern March Brown but similar timing + technique.

name: Callibaetis (Stillwater Speckled Spinner)
notes: Western stillwater signature. Hebgen Lake "gulpers" — cruising rainbow + brown trout sip duns on calm summer mornings.

name: Damselfly (Stillwater)
notes: Damsel nymph migration on Western lakes — trout key on them swimming to shore. Adult flights produce surface action when grasshoppers are slow.

name: Hex (Western)
notes: Western Hex emergence — Fall River CA + Hat Creek + Williamson. Big-fly evening game on stillwater + slow rivers.

name: Cicada (Western)
notes: Annual Western cicada emergence on the Green River below Flaming Gorge is legendary — May-June trophy brown trout key on terrestrial cicadas. Snake River + Yampa too.

name: Western Hopper
flies: Size 12 Beetle (black foam) — dropped under hopper
notes: Western hopper bite — July-September warm afternoons + wind. Bank seams + tall grass. Hopper-dropper rig (foam hopper + bead-head dropper) is the Western summer rig.

name: Terrestrial Ants + Beetles
flies: Size 14 Flying Ant (during summer ant swarms)
notes: Terrestrials are reliable summer searching patterns when no hatch is on. Drop a small ant or beetle in the foam line for surprise eats.

name: Western Flav (Small Green Drake)
notes: Yellowstone region "small green drake" — afternoon hatch on overcast summer days. Slough Creek + Lamar afternoon dries.

name: Winter Steelhead Run
rivers: Olympic Peninsula (Sol Duc, Hoh, Queets, Quinault)
notes: PNW winter chrome — December-April. Cold rain wading. Float-and-jig is the production method; Spey-swung Intruders are the prestige. Wild fish dominate.

name: Summer Steelhead Run
flies: Size 6 Hairwing dry (Muddler, Bomber, Greased Liner)
Size 8 October Caddis dry — searching pattern
notes: Summer steelhead — Spey-swung classics + dry-fly skating on Deschutes + North Umpqua. Fish hold months in the river before spawn.

name: Spring Chinook Run
flies: Kwikfish K15/K16 (chartreuse, fire tiger) wrapped with sardine
notes: Spring Chinook ("Springers") — March-June, ocean-fresh adults pushing inland to spawn. Premier table fare. Side-drifting roe + plug-pulling Kwikfish are the boats.

name: Fall Chinook Run
flies: Kwikfish K16 wrapped with sardine (fall colors — orange/silver)
notes: Fall Chinook — biggest salmon of the year. Lower-river fresh fish to upper-river dark/spawning. Hen + buck pairing visible on gravel.

name: Coho (Silver) Salmon Run
notes: Coho — most aggressive Pacific salmon. Chartreuse/pink wakes fish from holding. Smaller average than Chinook but better fight.

name: Sockeye (Red) Salmon Run
flies: Bare red hook with thread butt — Sockeye flossing rig
notes: Sockeye — plankton eaters that don't aggressively feed in freshwater. Flossing/lining is the technique. Brilliant red flesh; best table salmon.

name: Pink (Humpy) Salmon Run
notes: Pink salmon — odd-year (2021, 2023, 2025) explosive runs in WA + AK. Hump-backed males in spawning colors. Pink/pink/pink wins.

name: Chum (Dog) Salmon Run
flies: Size 4 Chartreuse + Hot Pink Bunny Leech
notes: Chum — late-fall run, often dark fish in heavy spawning colors. Aggressive bite on bright flies. Less revered than Chinook/Coho but great sport.

name: Light Cahill (Eastern/Midwest)
notes: Eastern + Midwest evening summer hatch — pale cream-yellow mayfly. Reliable summer dry on Smokies + Pennsylvania limestone streams.

name: Mysis Shrimp (Tailwater Forage)
notes: Mysis shrimp — released from dam outflows below Western reservoir tailwaters. Trophy trout below Ruedi (Frying Pan), Dillon (Blue), Taylor Park, Williams Fork, Flaming Gorge gorge themselves. Year-round forage.

name: Sowbug + Scud (Western)
notes: Sowbug + scud — year-round Western tailwater forage. Bighorn (orange + tan), Missouri (pink + olive), San Juan (red + orange), Green (orange + tan).

name: Western Streamer Bank (Browns + Trophy)
notes: Western streamer game — pre-spawn fall brown trout + post-spawn spring + high-water windows. Bigger flies = bigger fish. Sink-tip lines for depth.

name: Egg Patterns (Western)
notes: Egg patterns — fall/winter post-spawn forage. Streamer dropped behind a brown-trout pre-spawn buck eats the loose eggs.

name: Spey + Intruder Patterns (Steelhead)
flies: Size 4 OPST Commando Head Tube Fly
notes: PNW Spey + Intruder canon — Skagit-style + Scandi-style swung flies for chrome winter + dry-line summer steelhead.

name: Pacific Salmon Trolling + Bait Patterns
notes: Boat-fishery Pacific salmon gear. Side-drifting roe, plug-pulling Kwikfish, downrigger trolling. Distinct from fly-rod side of the fishery.

name: Desert Reservoir Bass Flies
flies: Size 1/0 Game Changer (Blane Chocklett) — chartreuse/white, smelt
Size 1/0 Pencil Popper Fly — bluegill, shad
notes: Western desert reservoir bass + striper fly fishing — Mead/Powell/Mohave/Havasu canyons. Big chartreuse-white flies, sinking line, retrieve. Stripers chase like trout in trophy zones.

name: Pacific Coast Saltwater Flies
flies: Size 2 Surf Perch fly (small, sand-crab imitation)
Size 4 Albacore Tuna Surface Plug fly (Cam Sigler)
notes: Pacific Coast surf + nearshore fly fishing — surfperch + striper (CA) + halibut + sharks + albacore. Heavy 8–10 wt rods, fast-sinking lines.

name: Pyramid Lake Lahontan Cutthroat Flies
flies: Size 4–8 Maroon Wooly Worm — Pyramid signature
Size 6 Marabou Streamer (white, black, olive, maroon)
notes: Pyramid Lake — fly anglers fish from "ladders" (step-up platforms in the surf) with two flies trailing. Maroon Wooly Worm + Beetle is the iconic Pyramid two-fly rig. Trophy 20–25+ lb Lahontan cutthroat trout.

name: Arctic Grayling Flies (Big Hole, Yellowstone NP)
notes: Arctic Grayling — rare native Montana fish, last reproducing population in the lower 48 (Big Hole). C&R on Big Hole. Small-fly attractor dries.
searchTerm: Arctic Grayling Flies (Big Hole, Yellowstone NP)

name: Carp Flies (Universal)
notes: Universal carp fly bank — sight-cast tailing carp in shallow water. Mulberry season (May) for berry patterns; ant + hopper for terrestrial windows; worm + crayfish year-round.

name: Smallmouth Bass Flies (Universal)
notes: Universal smallmouth fly bank — works on bronzeback fisheries from Susquehanna to Ozarks to Western rivers.

name: Panfish + Bream Fly Bank (Universal)
flies: Size 8–12 Foam Popping Bug (yellow, chartreuse, black)
Size 12 Bream Killer (chenille body, marabou tail)
notes: Universal bream + bluegill + redear + longear fly bank — full-moon May/June spawn beds, summer evening surface bite.

name: Striped Bass Flies (Inland Reservoir + Tailwater + Coastal)
notes: Striper fly bank — inland reservoir + tailwater + Atlantic coast + CA SF Bay + AZ Lake Mead. Match the bait: shad, herring, alewife, mullet.

name: Catfish Baits + Rigs (Universal)
flies: Cut shad (fresh) on Santee Cooper rig — trophy blues
Cut skipjack (fresh) on Santee rig — trophy blues
Stink bait (Sonny's, Sure Shot, Junnie's) — channel cats
Punch bait (Magic Bait, CJ's) — channel cats
Shrimp on circle hook — channel cats
notes: Universal catfish bait bank — different baits for different species. Blue + channel cats eat cut bait + scent; flatheads eat live bait + occasionally cut.

name: Walleye Lures + Trolling
notes: Universal walleye gear bank — bottom-bouncer crawlers for boats, slip-bobber leeches in calm water, jerkbaits along weed edges, jigging in winter ice.

name: Muskie + Pike Flies (Universal)
flies: Size 4/0 Bull Dawg (Musky Innovations) — natural, sucker, fire-tiger
Size 1/0 Schmidt Rattlesnake (Manistee/Hooch classic) — small muskie
Spinnerbaits + Bucktails (Mepps Musky Killer #6, Cowgirl) — gear-side
notes: Universal Esox fly + lure bank — Bull Dawgs are the standard; Polar Magnus + Buford bigger; bucktails (gear) on figure-8 at boatside.

name: Crappie Jigs + Live Bait Bank

Tube Jig — Strike King Mr Crappie
notes: Universal crappie bank — long-pole jigging brush, spider-rig open water, slip-cork minnows. Bobby Garland color codes are the crappie gospel.

name: Ice Fishing Jigs + Lures (Northern States)
flies: Tungsten Ice Jig 3mm — wax worm / spike (pan-fish)
Dead-stick rod + minnow on plain hook
notes: Universal ice-fishing bank — tungsten micro-jigs for panfish, jigging spoons for predators, tip-ups for pike/walleye. Flasher (Marcum, Vexilar) essential.

name: Hendrickson (Eastern)
notes: Hendrickson — Eastern spring hatch, late April. Females have red bodies; males ("Red Quill") have rusty bodies. Catskill mayfly classic.

name: Quill Gordon (Eastern)
notes: Quill Gordon — earliest major Eastern mayfly hatch. Late March / early April on Smokies; mid-April on Catskills.

name: March Brown (Eastern)
notes: March Brown — Eastern late-spring mayfly. Big bug (#10-12). Tan/cream body.

name: Isonychia (Slate Drake / Mahogany Dun)
notes: Iso — Eastern summer big mayfly, evening hatch. Swimming nymph type (active emergence).

name: White Fly Summer Mayfly (Eastern)
notes: White Fly — late-August Eastern summer spinner fall at dark. Yougheheny + Cumberland tailwater + others famous for it.

name: Yellow Drake (Eastern)
notes: Yellow Drake — Eastern late-June evening mayfly. Cream-yellow body.

name: Brown Drake (Eastern + Midwest)
notes: Brown Drake — June evening big mayfly. Catskills + MI rivers.

name: Green Drake (Eastern)
notes: Eastern Green Drake (Ephemera) — different species than Western Green Drake (Drunella). PA Penns Creek + Little Juniata famous.

name: Cranefly (Eastern + Universal)
notes: Crane flies — universal summer evening pattern. Skated cranefly dries trigger aggressive eats on Western rivers.

name: Florida-Strain Largemouth Fly Bank
notes: Florida-strain largemouth flies — big-fly bass on the fly. Pad/grass topwater for sight-cast trophy fish on Toho, Okeechobee, Stick Marsh.

name: Redfish Sight-Cast Fly Bank
notes: Sight-cast redfish fly bank — weedless gold spoonfly is the GA/Carolina flood-tide signature; EP Crab + Merkin for crab-eating reds; baitfish patterns for cruisers.

name: Great Lakes Steelhead Fly Bank
flies: Size 8 Glo Bug egg (chartreuse, peach, oregon-cheese)
Size 4 Marabou Tube Fly (Intruder-style) — Great Lakes lighter
notes: Great Lakes steelhead fly bank — egg, worm, stonefly nymph for indicator/center-pin; Spey patterns + leeches for swung-fly anglers. Manistee + Muskegon + Salmon River + Cattaraugus.

name: Driftless Spring Creek Trout (WI/MN/IA)
flies: Size 12 Wooly Bugger (olive, black) — small streams
notes: The Driftless Area — limestone spring creeks of WI/MN/IA/IL. Wild brown trout (and brookies in headwaters). Year-round opportunity; technical small-stream fishing.

name: Midwest River Smallmouth + Trout
notes: Midwest river smallmouth — Wisconsin, St. Croix, Ozark rivers. Wisconsin + Driftless brown trout in cooler reaches.

name: New England Stream Trout
notes: New England trout — Catskills hatch sequence (Hendrickson, Cahill, Quill Gordon, March Brown). Wild brook trout in ME/NH/VT headwaters; stocked rainbows and browns in tailwaters.

name: Northeast Coast Striper + Bluefish
notes: Northeast striper + bluefish — surf, jetty, boat. Match the bait: sand eels, peanut bunker, silversides, herring, mullet (fall run). Striper migration NJ to Maine April-November.

name: Texas Largemouth + Smallmouth Bass Flies
notes: Texas bass — Lake Fork, Falcon, Sam Rayburn, Toledo Bend. Big fly bass fly fishing on Florida-strain trophy lakes. Pre-spawn February (south TX) to April (north).

name: Texas Coastal Redfish + Trout
notes: Texas coast redfish fly fishing — Laguna Madre, Aransas Pass, Galveston Bay flats. Same playbook as GA/Carolina Golden Isles.

name: Louisiana Marsh Redfish
notes: Louisiana marsh redfish — pre-spawn fall bull reds 25–40+ lb. Larger flies than typical redfish work. World-class sight-fishing.

name: Plains States Walleye + Bass + Pike
notes: Plains states — Missouri River walleye + Devils Lake ND multi-species + Glendo Reservoir + Lake McConaughy. Plus river pike + smallmouth + bass + catfish.

name: Alaska Salmon + Trout Fly Bank
scientific: All five Pacific salmon + native rainbow + Dolly Varden + grayling
flies: Size 6 Glo Bug egg (chartreuse, pink, peach)
Size 14 Mouse Pattern (for rainbows on Kenai)
notes: Alaska — all five Pacific salmon + native rainbow + Dolly Varden + grayling. Beads pegged above the hook for trout-eating-salmon-eggs is the trophy bow technique.

name: Cape Cod Albies + Bonito + Bluefish
scientific: False albacore + bonito + bluefish (Cape Cod fall run)
notes: Cape Cod fall run — albies + bonito + bluefish blitzing peanut bunker schools. Fast strip retrieve on 8-9 wt rod. October peak.

name: Ozark Trout Tailwater (MO + AR)
flies: Size 4 Sex Dungeon (small) — brown trout streamer
notes: Ozark tailwaters — White, Little Red, Norfork, Eleven Point. Year-round dam-discharge cold water. State-record brown trout from Little Red.

name: New England Coastal Saltwater Bank
notes: New England saltwater — Cape Cod, Long Island Sound, Buzzards Bay, Narragansett, Boston Harbor. Stripers + blues mid-spring through fall. Fluke + scup + sea bass + tautog.

name: Maine Brook Trout + Landlocked Salmon
notes: Maine brook trout + landlocked salmon — smelt-imitating streamers (Black Ghost, Gray Ghost) for big-river salmon; small attractor dries for brook trout ponds + rivers.

name: Alaska Rainbow Trout (Kenai-Style)
flies: Size 1/0 Articulated Sculpin (Galloup's Sex Dungeon, white/yellow)
notes: Trophy Alaska rainbow trout — eats salmon eggs + flesh through July-September. Beads pegged 1-2 inches above the hook is the system. Mouse fishing on Kenai-style streams legendary.

name: Alaska Dolly Varden + Grayling
notes: Alaska Dolly Varden — char that mob salmon eggs + flesh. Arctic grayling on tiny dries + spinners. Subsistence + sport.

name: Plains Multi-Species Bank (KS/NE/SD/ND)
flies: Bucktail (1-2 oz) — striper on Lake McConaughy
notes: Plains states multi-species — Lake Sakakawea / Lake Oahe (SD) for walleye + bass; Devils Lake (ND) for walleye + perch + pike; Lake McConaughy (NE) for walleye + striper. Winter ice fishing major.

name: Louisiana Marsh Inshore Fly Bank
scientific: Redfish + speckled trout + black drum + flounder
notes: Louisiana marsh inshore fly bank — bull reds + slot reds + specks + black drum + flounder + tarpon (summer). Best inshore sight-fishing in North America.

name: Mid-Atlantic Saltwater (DE / MD / VA / NJ)
scientific: Striped bass + flounder + sea bass + tautog + bluefish
notes: Mid-Atlantic saltwater — Delaware Bay striper, Chesapeake migratory rockfish, NJ surf, VA Eastern Shore. Spring + fall runs are the peak.

name: Vermont + New Hampshire Wild Brook Trout
notes: VT + NH wild brook trout — small Catskill-style streams + Battenkill brown trout. Tight casts + small flies. Native brookies in headwaters.

name: Rhode Island Saltwater (Narragansett Bay)
notes: Rhode Island — Narragansett Bay striper, Block Island fall albie run, surf fishing on the South County beaches.

name: Atlantic Shoreline Surf + Pier Fly Bank
flies: Size 1/0 Surf Candy (clear epoxy, sand-eel shape)
notes: Atlantic shoreline surf + pier fly + gear bank — Maine to Florida. Striper migration corridor in spring + fall. Pompano + drum + blues + albies + sea bass + tautog + flounder all hit.

name: Plains Catfish + Walleye + Carp (Multi-Species)
flies: Cut shad on Santee rig (blue cats)
Chicken liver on circle hook (channel cats)
notes: Plains states gear + bait bank — Missouri River system + Lake Sakakawea + Lake Oahe + Devils Lake + Lake McConaughy + Tuttle Creek + Cheney + Milford. Walleye + pike + catfish + carp the staples.

name: Southern New England Brown Trout + Carp
scientific: Brown trout + carp + smallmouth (Southern New England)
notes: Southern New England — Farmington River trophy brown trout tailwater, Housatonic wild brown trout + smallmouth, Westfield River wild trout. Quality tailwater + freestone mix.

name: Universal Spinner + Spoon Gear Bank
flies: Panther Martin #2-4 (yellow body w/ red dots)
notes: Universal gear-side bank — inline spinners, casting spoons, suspending plugs, PowerBait. Stocked-trout staples, smallmouth searching, pike/walleye, panfish. Works on virtually every US fishery.

name: Universal Shore Bait Fishing Bank
flies: Sand fleas (mole crabs) on Pompano rig
Live mud minnows (mummichogs) on Carolina rig
Live finger mullet on circle hook
Live eel on egg-sinker rig (striper, fluke)
Live spot or croaker on circle hook (cobia, drum)
Dead anchovy on weight-forward rig (halibut PNW)
notes: Universal shoreline bait bank — works on the Atlantic, Gulf, Pacific. Match the bait + tide + species. Bottom rigs, Carolina rigs, popping corks, live bait drop-shots.

name: Alaska Saltwater (Halibut + Salmon + Rockfish + King Crab)
flies: Downrigger trolling herring on Coyote spoon
notes: Alaska saltwater — Homer Spit halibut + Cook Inlet king salmon + Prince William Sound silvers + Southeast bottom fishing. Charter capital of the world.

name: Universal Light-Tackle Spinning + Bait Bank
notes: Universal light-tackle spinning + bait — kid fishing, panfish ponds, small-river trout, walleye finesse. Covers VT, SD, ND, NE, DE light-pressure fisheries.
